text:
This was a couple of months ago now, but it’s relevant to what is going on around social media, Pauline Hanson, misinformation, fake news, AI-produced content & overseas accounts that generate the majority of this rubbish.

‘Research from ABC News Verify & The Guardian confirms that many large, seemingly grassroots pro-One Nation Facebook groups operate as foreign-run "meme factories". These networks are managed by offshore administrators in Southeast Asia (such as Vietnam & the Philippines) & are designed to monetize Australian online engagement through ad revenue rather than legitimate political support’

The abc news have identified at least 20 foreign-run pages with many linked to admins based in Sri Lanka, Vietnam & The Philippines. These pages rely on high volumes of AI-generated content - deepfakes, bogus articles, fabricated stories about political figures to drive outrage and generate interest.

The pages exist to game algorithms & direct traffic to external sites, which are profit driven.

The same techniques are used via foreign  Facebook accounts using an AI Pauline Hanson to manipulate Australian users. Posts involving Hanson are generally AI images with a small number of genuine pictures that depict Hanson in a positive & formative light in events that have never happened. Philanthropic roles in the community depict Hanson as a caring individual giving money away & helping people. Other “news stories” depict Hanson taking on other politicians - usually Anthony Albanese - painting the PM in a bad light.

All the so-called news & events that are generated have never happened - but these events & imagery are being liked &  shared throughout social media thousands of times by users who believe what they see without question & can’t see past the fake stories & imagery - these people aren’t prepared to research factual information that would negate this outside of the platform. Simple online searches would result in confirmation that these stories & events are not present in real life or in any news sources outside of the online platform source.

While Pauline Hanson has primarily been depicted in a positive light - Albanese has been depicted in negative events, argumentative and duplicitous. None of these events are true.

These fake news and events posts are used to engage users, encouraging emotive responses - and are more likely to induce anger and resentment. 

According to The Guardian Australia, pro One Nation facebook groups appear to be run by foreign meme factories that monetise content. 

‘Guardian Australia examined 14 of the largest pro-One Nation public groups with at least 8,000 members, and found most were created this year.

While some groups appear to be longstanding and set up by genuine supporters, the majority are full of content overwhelmingly fed by what digital media researcher Timothy Graham said appeared to be “a foreign-run, predominantly Indonesian, for-hire engagement farm operation”

Much of the content across these groups is designed to be what Graham called outrage or “poll bait” – asking yes or no questions, such as “Was Pauline Hanson right to scold this journo?” or “Should Sharia law be banned in the Australia?”
Other posts are reactive, with some of the accounts Guardian Australia tracked posting multiple times about the party’s “Fire the Liar” campaign. In at least one case, the text and image promoting the party’s fundraising drive was copied from a verified One Nation page’

So, pro Hanson content on social media driven by foreign players for positive pro-Hanson political content that is entirely fictitious….

I wonder who is driving this and how much of it is being paid for by Australian taxpayers?

It would appear there is much to be gained for some but not others via online fake media stories, memes and AI.

How to use Claude in 25 different ways:

(takes 60 seconds to read, saves you 60 hours)

1. Bookmark this image. You'll need it tomorrow.
2. Full guide + prompts at: https://t.co/CMWuI4VO4N.
3. Don't pay anything. It's free in the email.

---

1. ASK → Chat
☑️ Quick questions. Quick answers.
☑️ Don't use Cowork for "write a good email?"

2. THINK → Opus 4.8
☑️ The smartest model. Always switch to it.
☑️ For deep work, complex writing & thinking.

3. SPEED → Sonnet 4.6
☑️ Faster, cheaper, still excellent.
☑️ Use it for grammar, summaries, short answers.

4. REASON → Extended Thinking
☑️ Toggle it on for hard problems.
☑️ Turn it off for simple ones (saves tokens).

5. BUILD → Cowork
☑️ Where serious work happens. .docx, .xlsx, .pptx.
☑️ Point it at a folder. It works like an employee.

6. SHIP → Claude Code
☑️ For developers (and curious non-devs).
☑️ Build websites and apps from your terminal.

7. MODEL → Claude in Excel
☑️ Install the add-in from the Excel store.
☑️ Real formulas, real models, no copy-paste.

8. SLIDES → Gamma connector
☑️ One prompt. Full presentation. With research.
☑️ Connect Gamma inside Claude.

9. MOCKUP → Claude Design
☑️ Go to claude .ai/design. Build websites in 1 hour.
☑️ Upload a DESIGN .md. Export to Canva, PDF.

10. BROWSE → Claude in Chrome
☑️ A browsing agent. Reads any page for you.
☑️ Acts inside the browser, not just talks about it.

11. CLICK → Computer Use
☑️ Settings > Turn it on.
☑️ Claude clicks, types, browses on your Mac.

12. REMOTE → Dispatch
☑️ Connect your phone. Text a task.
☑️ It works on your computer while you're away.

13. SEARCH → Web Search
☑️ Click the '+'. turn it on.
☑️ It reads the live web before answering.

14. INVESTIGATE → Research
☑️ For deep multi-source reports.
☑️ 13 minutes. 50+ sources. One markdown file.

15. TRIGGER → Skills
☑️ "Use the skill-creator to build a skill for [task]."
☑️ Type /skill. It fires automatically.

16. STACK → Plugins
☑️ Cowork > Customize > Browse Plugins.
☑️ Bundles of skills for marketing, legal, sales.

17. PERSIST → Projects
☑️ One Project per recurring deliverable.
☑️ Upload one gold-standard example.

18. AUTOMATE → Scheduled Tasks
☑️ Every Monday at 7am, Claude runs your report.
☑️ Set it up once. No manual work. 

19. CONNECT → Connectors
☑️ Gmail, Drive, Slack, Notion, Granola, Calendar.
☑️ 50+ apps. Claude reads, writes & acts inside.

20. RENDER → Artifacts
☑️ Side pane where Claude opens charts, docs.
☑️ Interactive. Editable. Shareable.

21. VOICE → Custom Styles
☑️ Set "Concise" or build your own.
☑️ Saves you from re-explaining your voice.

22. SHARE → Team Projects
☑️ Same Project. Whole team inside.
☑️ Skills, files, instructions. Shared once.

23. GUARD → Global Instructions
☑️ Settings > Cowork > Global Instructions.
☑️ Tell Claude what to always do.

24. ORGANIZE → Obsidian Vault
☑️ Free app. Opens your Cowork folder neatly.
☑️ Edit files like Google Docs.

25. BRIEF → about-me file.
☑️ One md. file. Under 2,000 words.
☑️ Your style, your rules, your voice

Follow @markstrong_07

Hasn’t the US already outspent China on AI by a factor of 10? China recently announced $295B capex fund over 5 years, which is less than half of what the US spends in 2026. It’s prob more in PPP terms but US AI capex is clearly not constrained by the short-sightedness of financial media panicking. 

AI buildout in the US, if it's been constrained at all, it's certainly not by the formidable cost or investor and media pressure, but by the physical limitations of power, land and groundswell resistance. Fundamentally, this speaks to America’s limited AI supply elasticity, esp when trying to do so much in a hurry. 

Therein lies the problem. The US has a supply elasticity problem. Most of the hardware (chips, memory, networking) is imported by ally countries also with limited supply elasticities. Domestic grid is dilapidated and ramping up power generation has been a struggle. 

And the inflation pressure that ensues from AI capex also speaks to supply elasticity as AI competes with other use cases over the same input. Inflation also in turn worsens groundswell local opposition. 

China on the other hand has ample supply elasticity, which has become a signature strength due to the extensive, deep and flexible domestic supply chain. It also has the world’s most advanced ultra high voltage electrical grid network. Power isn’t a constraint for China. China’s constraint is advanced GPUs and EUV machines that have been embargoed. 

China has also been further constrained by the fact that AI is just a super expensive and speculative bet with unknown or highly uncertain ROI. If we leave aside govt's push to build out China's own chip industry that started much earlier, China's AI effort so far has been mostly private sector driven.

China's risk capital and investors have lower risk tolerance than their American counterparts and don't want to commit capital to projects without clear path to ROI. Until recently, China incl its tech firms have been watching the US aggressive push into AI somewhat skeptically even as it makes its own push following the US's footsteps in typical fashion. 

So I think this idea of AI as a capex arms race, which attractively maps to the US-USSR Cold War competition, is invalid for the present context. The US and China are running very different races with different conditions. The fact that China is keeping up while doing almost the same things much more efficiently and cheaply seems to be driving folks here a little mad. Always better to run your own race.

Fantastic visit to @riseholmecampus today! 

​This place is an absolute hidden gem of hands-on education. I was completely blown away by their incredible use of AI, robotics, and advanced tech. 

Getting to try out their cutting-edge VR setup and Virtual Tractor Simulation was just mind-blowing! 

I also loved jumping on a real tractor... and I am very pleased to report I didn't write either of them off!
​
Whether it is testing out VR gardening or visiting their brilliant animal zoo, everything they do here is about giving students real, practical experience.
​
Seeing this in action proves why we need to completely smash the academic snobbery in this country. It is total nonsense that people look down on vocational learning. 

We cannot expect students to sit at desks for six hours straight just memorising facts when they could be gaining real, common-sense skills for the real world.

​Riseholme is showing everyone exactly how it should be done. 

They are so committed to opening doors that they even spend around a million pounds each year, funding buses to get students to college to learn these vital trades. 

Let's get on with backing real skills for the next generation! 🇬🇧

🌸 Alpha - Fragments of Growth

A decade of travel, memories, and textures - now brought on-chain.

Created by fashion photographer and visual artist Jenya Vyguzov, the collection transforms textures captured across years of travel into handcrafted collage flowers , each petal representing a place, memory, or moment. No AI, no generative outputs, every piece is individually composed by hand.

What stands out here is the artist behind it. Jenya has spent over a decade working with brands like Vogue, GQ Style, Tatler, Elle, Puma, and IKEA, while building a distinctive visual language. His work was even selected for the official Paris Prints Textile Show poster, where he was given an entire floor for a solo exhibition.

⚠️ WL applications are coming soon. If you're interested in art NFTs, this is the stage where you want to be paying attention. Follow the artist and the project, turn on notifications, and be ready when WL applications open.

🔎Mint Details TBA

dyor/nfa

Andrej Karpathy explains why RLHF needed a cheat code.

The naive version sounds simple: show humans model outputs and ask them to score which ones are good.

Then he does the math:

"I want to do 1,000 updates, where each update will be on 1,000 prompts, and for each prompt we're going to have 1,000 rollouts that we're scoring."

"I will need to ask a human to evaluate a joke a total of 1 billion times."

"That's a lot of people looking at really terrible jokes, so we don't want to do that."

The trick is indirection:

"We're going to involve humans just a little bit, and the way we cheat is that we basically train a whole separate neural network that we call a reward model."

That is the hidden abstraction behind RLHF:

Humans do not scale the feedback loop.
A model trained to imitate human preference scales it.

As a PhD student, I was told not to work on deep RL - too full of hacks and alchemy" But after a year or two of working in this area, I’ve come to (deeply?) appreciate all of the thoughtful research that’s gone into understanding what/why things work and how to make them better.

My lab (joint with @abhishekunique7) took what we’ve learned by reading this body of literature to answer the question: what are the actual best-practices for finetuning a diffusion/flow/generative robot policy (for now, in sim)? Under one set of constraints - ample compute but limited time on your robot - @servo97  paper gives a pretty compelling answer.
